## [00:00 - 03:04]

So we've just seen the video of looking at Shopify data and connecting your UTM tracking, trying to find some insights on what's going on. I want to show you how you can also do that within Google Analytics. So what do you need for this? You need to make sure, obviously Google Analytics is installed properly. Number two, make sure your UTM tracking is connected to your ads. Number three, make sure enhanced tracking is turned on in Google Analytics e-commerce because that passes the product data to Google Analytics. If you go into Google Analytics under conversions, e-commerce and product performance, you'll end up with a page like this. On the left-hand side, you have the products, your revenue, et cetera, et cetera. And the cool thing about this is all the data we're pumping in through UTM tracking, it can be used pretty much in most, if not all of the reports in Google Analytics. How far you take that is entirely up to you. I don't think I'll have the time to go through every single possibility of how you can use this, but I want to ensure that you understand how and then you can find out where to use it that's most relevant for you. So for example, if we're looking at products and we want to look at which campaigns or which ad sets or which ads are driving sales into which products, then you can do it through here as well. So for example, if I want to list campaigns and then view them by products, I can do it like this. So what I've done here is just to select the secondary dimension campaign, it's showing me listed by product revenue, the campaign values. So what you can see here is there aren't actually many values and that's not really useful for us. Now there's two ways that we can look at data just for Facebook. So first one is I've already created a segment here for Facebook ads only traffic. And you can see in the little pop-up box, medium contains CPC, which is our standard way of passing the medium parameter and source contains Facebook. That again relates to this data here. It doesn't matter what you put as long as it matches what you've put in. So if you call that FB or social ads or this is called ads or paid social, whatever, that's up to you. So just making sure that that data is set up properly enables you to then set this up properly. So you can create a segment up here as we've showed before or as I'm gonna do here, once you've selected your secondary dimension, which is campaign, you can click on advanced. And now for example, I wanna show campaign containing cold. Now one of the limitations with Shopify was that you have to have exactly is, you know, this is this full string of the campaign, it either is or isn't with Google Analytics, you can show partial matching. So it's can contains, you can do exact if you want. So for example, with our funnel, funnel structure we have cold, warm and hot. I can just type in cold and see what comes up.

## [03:04 - 06:04]

And then I can look at this data based on that, the product sales and looking at which campaigns are actually driving those sales as well. And as I've showed before, you can look at it by ad set by selecting the content parameter for secondary dimension. And also keyword down to the ad level. And again, the power of this, the way we would use this and the way we do use this is looking at the ads that we are serving and then the products that people are ultimately buying. This is most relevant for multi product stores. But also if you have multi variants as well, let's say for example, you've got bundles that you're running on your site, you can actually see what's being purchased in the data in Google Analytics. So there might be some other ways that you wanna track and see what people bought. Obviously, average order value would give you a hint as well. But this just allows you to have a look at that as well. And also by the way, Google Analytics is playing up today, as you can see here. That's all good. We can go back in and run that again. This does sometimes happen. This is quite a large account. So you can see for November we've done 600K already so far. Not the largest. There are sometimes some data delays. Often it's just, I don't know if it's Google's network or my network or something like that. But if you hit apply again, it should load up. So now that the data is loaded, I can see for any campaign that contains cold, what that full campaign name is. And you can see here, this was slightly set incorrectly. And what they're actually buying. And that's kind of broken down by the quantity purchased, order value, things like that. So what I'm trying to get across here is free commerce in particular, if you're looking at optimization opportunities, product performance in Google Analytics, as well as looking at that in Shopify, can help you to understand how to pitch your message and your creative. For example, let's say that you're running dynamic product catalog ads and you wanna see eventually what people are buying. You could, before iOS 14, look at the breakdown report of products and look at the revenue coming through. Now you can't see an ads manager, but you can see it in your campaigns here. So that should allow you to make better decisions. So for example, with your dynamic catalog, if certain products are more likely to be bought, you could rather than showing the whole catalog, show a sub catalog that contains your top products based on analysis on what people are clicking on your ads. ## [06:04 - 09:04]

let's say, medium, we'll do source medium. We can list this by just looking at particular channels. So here by default, it says, Clevier email at the top because that's the most popular path for this. We can change that to source medium contains Facebook ads and apply. And then that should give us the data specifically for Facebook ads. And again, we can look at this at the highest level to see which products are most popular through that particular channel. So this is not just about Facebook ads, it's also about the other channels and see where people are ultimately putting their money as well. So we can see here, for example, the top selling product on Facebook ads in this period is this product and second most popular, et cetera. So again, it's using the data and making decisions. So what do I do with this? I'm looking at my top products to make sure that I'm advertising those at the top of my funnel. So here's the thing, there'll be a lot of these ads. And again, you can bring back campaign and show by your bottom of funnel, which for us is a campaign name contains hot. And then I can look at the analysis there to see what people are buying. Cause ultimately, if I'm driving top of funnel ads, I might not always match the user's intent with the product they want to buy. I might be testing lots of different creatives and I might see that click through rate on this looks really good, but they're not buying. Maybe the click through rate is good because the product shown in the creative was what they wanted, but when they got to the lander, it was a different product or maybe it was a category page. And then if you actually looked at your click path data, maybe retargeting converted on your top performing products. Now, if you move that top performing product to the top of the funnel and advertise that, guess what happens? Click through rate will still be good, but conversion rate could improve. And they're the things that we're looking at tweaking and improving, especially in an account of this size. And honestly, it can work on any size of account. And we do this on our only common store, which is generally small spend. So, you know, some months we do 30K ad spend, some months we only do 10K, some months we might go higher. We use this for accounts of all sizes. So this account probably spends, I think, 20 to 30K a day on ads. And this kind of analysis, you're tweaking and grabbing any opportunity to get higher conversions, higher average order values, higher ROAS and lower CPS, but it applies for accounts at all levels. So this kind of level of tweaking, if you're only spending, let's say, 100 day on ads and you're generating 200 or $250 on sales, this would probably come a bit later on when you get more data.

## [09:04 - 09:10]

But, you know, certainly as you're starting to look at other opportunities to tweak and improve, this becomes so much more crucial.
